item.title: Zero-hour contract crackdown risks job losses, business warns
item.description: Trade bodies say a requirement to offer staff guaranteed time could be ‘tipping point’ in already weak labour market

item.title: FirstFT: Shooting suspect had written about targeting Trump, authorities say
item.description: Also in today’s newsletter: Netanyahu’s biggest rivals merge Israeli political parties, China’s Xiaomi targets European expansion

item.title: Michael Jackson biopic is hit with audiences despite critical backlash
item.description: ‘Michael’ earns $217mn on opening weekend in boon for Lionsgate, Universal and the late singer’s estate

item.title: Canadian red tape is worse than Trump tariffs, say industry groups
item.description: Excessive regulation is strangling Mark Carney’s efforts to kick-start economic growth, companies argue

item.title: Starbucks, Nashville and the new corporate geography
item.description: Big US companies are realising they can unbundle their headquarters

item.title: Major City reforms to be set out in King’s Speech
item.description: Legislative package will include financial services bill designed to complete several planned changes to regulation

item.title: UK car loan companies accept £9bn mis-selling redress scheme
item.description: Main industry finance trade body reverses course on legal challenge to FCA plan

item.title: Colombia hosts first meeting to quit fossil fuels as energy crisis worsens
item.description: ‘Coalition of the willing’ kicks off strategy talks despite absence of big emitters

item.title: UK high streets rebound as TikTok generation brings in-store buzz
item.description: Demand for prime locations rises after dismal Covid period

item.title: Why bank taxes might be back on the table in the UK
item.description: The problem with ‘windfall’ taxes is that the relatively small amount raised is often not worth the long-term cost

item.title: Can AI discriminate if it can’t justify itself?
item.description: Elon Musk’s lawsuit against Colorado raises a deeper philosophical question about artificial intelligence and democracy

item.title: Google banks on AI edge to catch up to cloud rivals Amazon and Microsoft
item.description: Thomas Kurian, Google Cloud’s CEO, says its AI chips and models can help the data centre business gain ground

item.title: Jeff Bezos’s AI lab in talks over taking London office space at King’s Cross
item.description: Project Prometheus would be latest AI group to take space in UK capital amid global expansion

item.title: Iran war hits pistachio supplies amid Dubai chocolate boom
item.description: Prices for the nuts have surged after disruption to export routes from Iran, a major producer of the nut

item.title: LinkedIn’s Ryan Roslansky: ‘No one is taking care of your career for you’
item.description: Co-author of ‘Open to Work’ argues AI will bring opportunity — if workers are brave enough to take the initiative

item.title: Fuel crisis hits remote Pacific island nation of Tuvalu
item.description: Energy crunch triggered by Middle East war reaches shores of Polynesian country

item.title: China’s smartphone king takes on Elon Musk in Europe with premium EVs
item.description: Xiaomi wants to expand on the continent as demand for its cars outstrips production

item.title: Vladimir Putin’s regime turns on book publishers
item.description: Kremlin crackdown targets even loyal publishing houses

item.title: Good news for business travellers
item.description: The seats in economy that so many of us use are finally getting an upgrade

item.title: Companies with Mythos access urge joint defence of infrastructure
item.description: Cyber security chiefs warn rollout of new tool will need close co-ordination between governments and businesses

item.title: EV ownership at ‘tipping point’ in many parts of the world, experts say
item.description: Data shows momentum shift under way in Europe and emerging markets

item.title: Defence stocks give back gains as investors buy rumour but sell war
item.description: Production bottlenecks and uncertainty over US munitions funding weigh on stock prices for weapons makers

item.title: Marketing collective The Independents explores $1bn stake sale
item.description: Paris-based group in talks with its investors amid slump in high-end market

item.title: Chelsea’s troubles and the limits of ‘smart money’ in sport
item.description: Also in today’s newsletter, Premier League managerial churn and a brutal NY Mets losing streak

item.title: What the AI ‘jobpocalypse’ narrative misses
item.description: Whether or not new technologies can perform a task is only a tiny part of the picture

item.title: Iran seeks to resume flights from bomb-damaged airports
item.description: Authorities are reopening travel hubs after Tehran suspended air traffic because of US-Israeli strikes

item.title: Banks brace for tax raid if Starmer is ousted
item.description: Executives prepare for shift to the left in UK government after narrow escape in last year’s Budget

item.title: America’s energy supremacy is being forged in war
item.description: The US stands to benefit as war in Iran reshapes oil and gas flows, but Europe and Asia are wary of becoming too reliant on American supply

item.title: From Anthropic’s Mythos to the Birkin bag, scarcity sells
item.description: The idea that Mythos is too hot to handle ought to be good for the company’s valuation

item.title: The incredible double life of a spyware salesman turned spy
item.description: He built a booming business selling spy gadgets to the paranoid and the powerful. Behind the scenes he was wiring his own world
